title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] LOGAN KILPATRICK: Awesome. Welcome to day two of I/O, first
I/O dialogue session coming off tons of incredible
announcements yesterday, led by most of the folks
who are on this stage. So I'm super excited
for this conversation. My name is Logan Kilpatrick. I'm a PM inside of DeepMind
working on developer stuff, working on Gemini. So I see a bunch of
familiar faces from folks who are hosting in the crowd. So thanks for being here. And I'm excited
to talk about all of the AI moment, the
behind-the-scenes stories of all the launches that we saw
today being made possible again by the team inside of
DeepMind and across Google. We'll kick off with
some super quick intros. Koray is our CTO in DeepMind,
been leading DeepMind for a long time now, one of
the leaders in the AI space in artificial intelligence. You're historically a research
scientist and engineer. So you come from that
background, so excited to get your perspective today. Liz leads our Search
team inside of Google. You've been at Google
for 22 years now, helping shepherd Search into
the AI and agentic era, which has been awesome to see. You also led Maps
before as well. Awesome. And Josh, making I/O happen. ELIZABETH REID: [LAUGHS] LOGAN KILPATRICK: Josh, I think
of you as the emcee of I/O almost. JOSH WOODWARD: No. LOGAN KILPATRICK: So I think we
need to codify that officially next year. But Josh, you lead the Gemini
app team and Google Labs. And Google Labs is responsible
for a bunch of the product launches that we saw yesterday. So we'll see and we'll talk
more about that later today. So I'm excited for
this conversation. Thank you all three for
taking the time to chat. Let's start off with this
thread around, Koray, for you, Gemini being built to
be natively multimodal. We hear this story a lot
about Gemini from the origin, with Gemini 1.0, it was
natively multimodal. It could take in images,
audio, video, et cetera. And I was listening to a podcast
that you were on last night. And you were saying that
this is fundamentally
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
with Gemini 1.0, it was
natively multimodal. It could take in images,
audio, video, et cetera. And I was listening to a podcast
that you were on last night. And you were saying that
this is fundamentally because we believe that
the way to get to AGI is by having a multimodal model. Taking that at face
value, when you think about the
opportunities for Google-- and we had some of these
that we announced yesterday where we create bespoke
independent of Gemini models-- can you talk about where the
balance is between those models and mainline Gemini? KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: Yeah. Thank you very much. First of all, great to
be here with everyone, welcome, and sharing the
stage with all of you folks. What you said is right. From the point of view of
building AGI and doing research in that direction, of course,
that multimodality is important. We've got to be able to get in
all the different perceptive streams into the model. And when we say multimodality,
sometimes we think about just like video, audio, text. But it's also more than that. Actions are there,
they are important. So we are thinking about
tool calls, function calls, these kinds of things that
are natural for agents, models to do, anything
that is in that spectrum. The models should be able to
take them input and output. And then about the
bespoke models, building on this,
when you do research, it's always important that
you explore something, you explore an idea. And that's always
critical to do that. You need, in a way, that
sanitary environment to push a particular
idea, figure out what works, what
doesn't, and prove that. But a big part of the
investment in the Gemini program has been to bring things
in as quickly as possible. And of course, it's a
challenge, because merging a lot of different threads
in one model, a model that is capable of doing coding
and text generation and image generation and
everything in one place. But you also start getting
the effect of the transfer between those different fields.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
a lot of different threads
in one model, a model that is capable of doing coding
and text generation and image generation and
everything in one place. But you also start getting
the effect of the transfer between those different fields. So I think my canonical example
for this is vibe coding. Vibe coding works
because you have a model that understands the
world so that you can really give a high-level description
of what you want to see, rather than a technical
description of what you want to see. And you can even
do that with voice. You can speak to the
model, say that, oh, I want an application that
can do this and this and this and that. And by the way, here's
a little sketch. And I want it to look like that. And then the model
can generate that. That capability, to me, is
where everything comes together. And that's why we keep
doing that, right? LOGAN KILPATRICK:
I have one quick follow up just to rehash
part of the conversation we were having back backstage. One of the announcements
from yesterday was this Diffusion
Gemini experiment. KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: Yeah. LOGAN KILPATRICK: And it's
truly a research experiment, as our conversation was
going through yesterday. How do you think about that
as an example of where we're doing experiments versus where
the mainline capabilities are going? KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: That's a
really exciting experiment. Because diffusion models
have been around for a while. All the image and video
generation models that we have are diffusion models. And doing text generation
with diffusion, I think it's something that
the researchers in AI field have been experimenting
with for a while. It's not that easy,
but it has advantages. The reason we are pushing
it as an exciting direction is it might have
its own advantages, like looking at a large
chunk of output space. And then to be able to, in
a way, go back in that space and correct the
mistakes and such. So for really complex
domains like coding, this might be an advantage. That's why we are
exploring that. And it's an exciting avenue.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
And then to be able to, in
a way, go back in that space and correct the
mistakes and such. So for really complex
domains like coding, this might be an advantage. That's why we are
exploring that. And it's an exciting avenue. Now the job for us is-- it is exciting. I think the demo
is very convincing. And we know that there is
something we can get there. But we have a bunch of steps
before we can comfortably bring it into the main
Gemini and make it available. That's what we are
working on right now. LOGAN KILPATRICK: And
one more quick follow-up to just help set the
context for this year's I/O and also just for the broader
conversation around what's happening in AI is around scale. And we had this conversation
as a broken record. We're always
talking about scale. There's scale of
model, progress, and the size of the models. There's the
infrastructure scale. How are you thinking about
the scaling from a DeepMind perspective? If you were making
an investment in, here's how to bucket
out the scale advantage that you want to try to create,
how are you thinking about that? KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: Look, there's
a lot of discussion about scale and what it means. When we look at
model performance, there are a bunch of
factors that go into that. And even scale is correlated
with many of them. But of course, I think
what is on top of mind when we talk about
scale for everyone is the scale of the
model, size of the model. And the way I think about
this is there is something that we know that we increase
the size of the model, it does better. That's a given, because you have
more computational performance there. Of course, if you think that is
the only way to improve things, then you basically end up
suppressing all the creativity that can come. So at least I can
speak for Gemini, what we try to do is have a very
broad spectrum of explorations in many things. So I try to think about
three main buckets. One is data. Data is really important. Data is not just about
getting data into the model.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
what we try to do is have a very
broad spectrum of explorations in many things. So I try to think about
three main buckets. One is data. Data is really important. Data is not just about
getting data into the model. It's also about, there's
a lot of creativity that you can put there. How do you get that data? How do you represent that data? What do you learn from
different kinds of data streams? What is the correlation between
those, especially when we go to multimodality? There's very important,
exciting research there. And then there's the
model architecture itself, the algorithm, the
optimization methods-- all those are very important. And in each one of
these, actually there's the scaling aspect. And then the next thing that
you have is inference time. When you think once
you have the model, then it's the inference time. So the techniques that
are in inference time are multiplicative in a way. Because you have a model
that has a certain capacity at that point. But you can train it to be a lot
more effective when it is being used to reason for longer. So there are many more areas,
but these three main buckets are, I think, a good way to
think about it-- data, model, inference time. And scale is one
aspect of research that you can do across all,
but there are many others. And algorithmic improvements
are actually quite critical. And a lot of the improvements
I think that are happening are coming from a
mix of everything. LOGAN KILPATRICK:
That's awesome. So I think this scale thread
goes perfectly to Search, Liz. And I think Search is such
a great example of where you have these really,
really tough scale problems at the size and
all the latency constraints. I'm curious what Gemini is
enabling in search today that, historically,
you wish 10 years ago, we would have been able
to do, but we just now have the technology
to actually make this possible at the
scale actually of Google Search, which is such
a challenging problem? ELIZABETH REID: I
think one thing that has been really
exciting is we started
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
have the technology
to actually make this possible at the
scale actually of Google Search, which is such
a challenging problem? ELIZABETH REID: I
think one thing that has been really
exciting is we started with this mission of organizing
all the world's information. But for a long time, your
ability to answer a question was kind of limited based on
whether or not the answer to it was all in one place. And so that sort of
forced you, as a human, if you had a multi-part
question, if you had five constraints, to basically
take the question and break it up into each
constraint and issue, like, OK, you're going camping
with five kids and a dog, and your in-laws, and
here's what you need. Then you'd be like,
"camping with the dog," "camping with kids,"
"camping with--" right? And so you would do all of this
work to break the question up, because you would think that
maybe nobody in the world wrote a web page just for you. And one of the things we're
able to do with Gemini, we call it the query
fan-out technique. We talked about
an AI Mode, and we use a simpler version of
this in AIO, in which you go and you say, OK, instead of you,
the user, having to do this, the model does this. So then the model goes
and says, actually, these are all the different pieces. And it queries the web, and then
it pulls all the information together. And so that ability to say,
we can now answer a question as long as the
information in its whole is somewhere on the
web, as opposed to in one spot on the web,
is really remarkable. That lets you ask a lot
of harder questions, but it also actually
lets you do something that I think is less
obvious to folks in the US, which is that the content
in various languages and in various countries is just
a lot smaller than it is here in the US and in English. And so previously, your
access to information was partly limited based
on the language you spoke. And now with the
models, you can ask the question in your
language, and we can find the content
in another language
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
And so previously, your
access to information was partly limited based
on the language you spoke. And now with the
models, you can ask the question in your
language, and we can find the content
in another language and we can transform it back
in a way you understand. And then if you
want to continue, you can go deeper on the web
using a tool like Translate to access that
web page and that. And so that sense that the
barriers to information cease to be things
like language, I think, is really incredible in
opening up new opportunities for people around the world. LOGAN KILPATRICK:
That's awesome. Josh, we are having the
conversation this morning about the future of I/O
and also just reflecting on this year's I/O and how it's
become very clear that Gemini is this thread that
brings all of Google together across all of our
products and Search and YouTube and for our enterprise
customers in Cloud and everywhere
else in all the lab stuff that your
team has shipped. Can you give us the
high-level of where you think, from a
Gemini app perspective, the Gemini app plays
in there and what the north star looks like? JOSH WOODWARD: I think
with the Gemini app, we've always been
interested-- and you heard us talk about it
yesterday too-- in what would it take to build a truly
universal AI assistant. And that's been something,
even if you go all the way back to the original
I/Os on stage, when we introduced
the Google Assistant, you'll find those
phrases are there. And I think what's exciting
about this moment is the models and the capabilities
are now at a point, just like Koray
was saying and even what Liz was saying,
where you can actually take a real shot at
building something-- we talk about it on the team--
that's personal, proactive, and powerful. And when we think about
those three P's, we want it to be personal. As much info as you want
to give it-- it's up to you how much you want to give
it-- but if you give it a lot of your other
information across Google, it can do useful stuff for you. That's the personal bit.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
As much info as you want
to give it-- it's up to you how much you want to give
it-- but if you give it a lot of your other
information across Google, it can do useful stuff for you. That's the personal bit. I think the proactive one is
something I'm so excited about. Because so much of AI
today is actually reactive. And so you have to ask
something and then it answers. But if it can
bring things to you at just the right moment,
that I think really changes the whole
user experience. And then I think the
powerful part, a lot of you hopefully saw some of this
stuff or you've gotten a chance to play with Veo 3. Have you had a chance to
try it in the Sandbox? It's amazing. And I think if you think
about video, imagery, coding, all these things coming together
in an assistant that knows you, that can anticipate
things, and you can give it tasks to help do,
that's what we're going to try to take a shot at. And there's some of the
stuff that landed yesterday. And we're rolling out
a whole bunch of stuff over the next coming weeks. LOGAN KILPATRICK: It's
been wonderful to see that vision come to life. And people are really
picking up on the three P's. So we were also joking
backstage that we need swag that talks
about the three P's, because I think it's
going to keep coming up, from a universal
assistant perspective. Liz, I think there's also been
this broader thread about just like what an AI-enabled
search looks like. And I'm curious, yesterday
when the rollout for AI mode started to all US users, it
feels like this huge step forward for search. And I'm curious just what
the behind-the-scenes of actually making that happen? And I have so much empathy
for you and the Search team. Because I feel like Search
has this incredibly important stewardship position
to play in the world and can't just go and
break a bunch of things. Because we really are the
front door for the internet. So I'm curious how you
think about that and also how the moment of rolling out
yesterday actually happened?
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
and can't just go and
break a bunch of things. Because we really are the
front door for the internet. So I'm curious how you
think about that and also how the moment of rolling out
yesterday actually happened? ELIZABETH REID: I think
there's a bunch of constraints, but you can also
view the constraints as space for creativity
and really think about it. I think one of the things we've
seen with gen AI in Search is both that it can
be extremely useful, but also the bar is high. People have a great experience
with a lot of things on Search. And so if you're going
to bring in gen AI, you can't be like,
aha, gen AI is cool, I'm just going to
add it to Search. It actually has to be, it's a
better experience for people. And so we've rolled
this out through a bunch of different ways. One is just really
paying attention to what really
matters to people. So things like,
is it trustworthy? How high accuracy is it? And is it helpful? Is it more helpful than
the rest of the page? Latency was a big thing. There are multiple times where
we wanted to roll out a bigger model on a slice of
queries, and we didn't pick the right slice of queries. Because what happened was
it was a better response, but the amount of additional
time it took to respond was not worth the added
value to the user. And there's not a
magic thing where users are willing to wait 500
milliseconds all of the time. It's like some things they
would spend 30 minutes doing. And so they'll give
you 5 seconds to do it. Because 30 minutes, 5 seconds,
it's a pretty good trade-off. Others, they're like, uh-uh,
you're past 1.2 seconds, I'm not going to
use you as much. And that may sound
like an exaggeration, but literally we would
see people's search usage vary based on getting
the quality and speed trade-off right. So we had to work on them. We were like, OK,
well, this model is too big for it or we
don't have the right size, but we can do it for math. OK, let's get a better model. And so we work closely
with Koray's team on trying to figure
out how you get
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
We were like, OK,
well, this model is too big for it or we
don't have the right size, but we can do it for math. OK, let's get a better model. And so we work closely
with Koray's team on trying to figure
out how you get the models that maximize that. You also think about
latency in multiple ways. Latency is partly this question
about, can you get started? So people only read so fast. So you don't have to have
the entire response available before someone
does start to read. You have to have confidence
that the first things you say are good. So you can't just
go and be like, I'm going to start talking. Oh, that was bad. Let me start over. And that really matters. But you want to think about
that latency then of, OK, well, what's the first
part I can give you? And then I can give you
additional information by the time you get there. And then it feels fluid. So there's all these little
subtleties in how you indicate. Things like bolding, which
I don't think the world's like, if you talk
to a normal person, they're like, why
does bolding matter? Bolding really matters
to people because bolding helps with efficiency. How quickly can
you skim and find the relevant part of that thing? And so there's a lot of both
core underlying technology. But there's a lot of
product craft on top of it to getting those little things
that really matter to people. And so we're
constantly both trying to push the boundaries on
the underlying foundations, but also working to
get those little things on the product that just make it
feel smooth and really helpful. And then Search becomes more
of a delight and effortless versus like it's a chore. KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: To jump in
quickly, what Liz said is true. All these models have
been great collaboration. And we learn a lot
from that as well. Because I think Search is the
greatest product in the world, and it has the strictest
constraints in a way. And what you said
is really true. Constraints are creativity. In an unconstrained
world, it's really hard
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
Because I think Search is the
greatest product in the world, and it has the strictest
constraints in a way. And what you said
is really true. Constraints are creativity. In an unconstrained
world, it's really hard to choose why do you do one
thing over the other one. So we use that a lot. And then this is actually
our [INAUDIBLE] frontier of trying to get higher quality,
faster, higher quality faster. And that's basically
what leads to models like Flash, Flash-Lite, and
more specialized things. So this is a great
example of how we try to push
technology forward, getting constraints and feedback
from users and end products. LOGAN KILPATRICK:
And, Liz, how do you think about
that relationship that Search has
with users in those cases where they're
willing to only wait one second versus cases
maybe where they'd be willing to wait hours? And you just launched Deep
Search inside of AI mode. And that's the experience
of much longer-term tasks that Search is
taking on for you. So how have you
thought about this sort of balance between those? ELIZABETH REID:
So technically, we announced Deep Search
is coming very soon. LOGAN KILPATRICK: Coming soon. ELIZABETH REID: But
not quite yet, in case you're all looking for it. But it is something that
we've had to pay attention to, both from a query slice, but
also how do you empower people? So AI Overview because
it's in main search has the strictest
latency expectation. But we have AI
mode when you want to have the more advanced
thinking and reasoning. But we still assume
that people, most of the time when they're using
that, want it pretty fast. And then it's like,
OK, you're going to go buy a new appliance for
the first time in 10 years. You would normally have
spent three hours on it. You're clearly willing to wait a
few minutes for an expert level report that gives
you great links. I don't know how many
of you have kids. I have three kids. Camp planning, it's
kind of a mess. They all have
different start times. And I'm willing to wait
quite a few minutes if you're
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
report that gives
you great links. I don't know how many
of you have kids. I have three kids. Camp planning, it's
kind of a mess. They all have
different start times. And I'm willing to wait
quite a few minutes if you're going to come back with
an answer that saves me hours and hours of work. And so that is really
that question about, can you give people
different options so that they can really
choose how much time they're trying to put in a task? And can you constantly
be sensitive to what is really the underlying task
the user is trying to do? Because the query they come
to you is sometimes the task. But oftentimes it's like a
proxy for the task, where they did some amount of the work. And so if you go back and you
step and say, what are you really trying to do? Then you can think about
new ways to solve it. LOGAN KILPATRICK: Liz,
I love this example that you give around just how
long is somebody willing to wait to do something or
how much time would it have taken them to do this. I think about a lot
of tasks, for me personally, where
they're like, you should spend three hours
looking at appliances. What actually happens is I just
go and find the second thing. And I'm like, OK, this
looks pretty good. I'm just going to do that. And my partner always
pokes fun at me for that. So it's also fascinating
to see these opportunities where AI is going far and beyond
what humans would normally have done for people who aren't
going to spend the time doing that, which is just awesome. ELIZABETH REID: I also
think that's a great example in that there are
people who will spend days and days double and
triple checking the toaster-- [LAUGHTER] --and others who
apparently do not. And so when you build a tool
that billions of people use, you need to make it work
for both Koray and Logan. And it's not a
one-size-fits-all. And I think that's what's
interesting about Gemini working on the universal
assistant being personal. I think that's what
we're going to continue to see in Search as well.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
And it's not a
one-size-fits-all. And I think that's what's
interesting about Gemini working on the universal
assistant being personal. I think that's what
we're going to continue to see in Search as well. We're talking about
being more personal. Personal isn't just, do you
have a better recommendation? It'll probably be like, how
hard do you want to work? How do you actually
want to learn? Do you want to learn
by audio or visual? Do you want to learn
on your way home? Or do you want to do it while
you have five minutes here? And how does that change? And we just have to adapt
because it's important that the tech
comes to the people and not the people
come to the tech. That's what had to happen
when the tech was limited. But as much as possible, we
should meet people the way that they want to engage,
not the way that we think is the best for them to engage. JOSH WOODWARD: And if I could
add one thing on top of that, I think, to tie back to
one of the things Koray said about Gemini being
natively multimodal. I was telling this to
a friend the other day who's not in Silicon Valley. They were asking me, what's
one of your biggest surprises over the last year working
on AI stuff every day? And the first thing
that came to mind was this idea that content
is now infinitely remixable. And just like Liz
is saying, you can have a set of things
that are documents that turn into a podcast
or a set of things that come in as images and
videos and everything else, and it turns into a
multi-step action plan. So I think this
idea-- especially as there's a lot of builders,
I recognize a few of you out here in the
audience-- as you're thinking about your
applications and the problems you're trying to solve
for your customers, I would just try to take that
as an assumption that's already mostly true today,
and just getting better and better and better,
this idea that you can remix content and weave it together. Because that's
just been something I've been really blown away how
each generation of the Gemini
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
mostly true today,
and just getting better and better and better,
this idea that you can remix content and weave it together. Because that's
just been something I've been really blown away how
each generation of the Gemini models just keeps
getting better at that. There's no end in
sight, from what we can see, say, on the Gemini
side or the Google Labs side. And it enables all kinds
of new experiences. LOGAN KILPATRICK: On this
thread of new experiences, when we landed Gemini 2.0,
one of the things that was so exciting about this was
that the model had native tool support. And one of the native tools
that Gemini models are actually fundamentally trained to
know when to use is Search. And Koray, I think now we're
having this really interesting moment where it's like
reasoning plus tools looks a lot like this thing is just
kind of an agent out of the box in a lot of ways. I'm curious the balance, from
a model training perspective, where we get the
model to use tools, where it doesn't use tools, how
that interacts with reasoning? Where's your head at? KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: That's
a really important point. I think, especially
the idea of tool calls, function calls, that's
the next big frontier. I think when you look around
the field, a lot of progress has happened in the
last six months on that. But I feel like we are just
scratching the surface. When I think about ourselves,
a big part of our daily lives is we use a lot of tools. We build a lot of tools,
and then we use those tools. We let others use those tools. There's a world in
which the models should create their own tools as well. We are just actually
enabling the models to use the tools that is around. And we started with Search. Of course, it's
the natural thing. We are at Google,
we have Search. It's amazing to be able to use
the knowledge, the freshness, the information about
world that is in Search and the model trying to figure
out when and how to use Search. So there's a lot of
research that went into it. But now I think it's
not perfect, of course.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
It's amazing to be able to use
the knowledge, the freshness, the information about
world that is in Search and the model trying to figure
out when and how to use Search. So there's a lot of
research that went into it. But now I think it's
not perfect, of course. But a lot of it was about, given
what the user conversation is, how can we figure out a way to,
OK, is this the right time to go and do a search and
get fresh information or factual information
from the web, rely on the search
technology that has built all that information,
and then integrate that into the response to the user? And of course, we
see the benefits of that in terms of factuality,
freshness of information. And then we started more and
more generalizing from there. But I think native
search integration is really critical for Gemini. I think it's an important
factor, to be able to do that. But in general, the tool
use and function calls, I think that's really a big
frontier that is really, really exciting right now. LOGAN KILPATRICK: And I promised
I would put you in the hot seat. So I've got another

models and they spit out tokens and that's the extent
to what they do. I think you can imagine the

from a future that looks more like that versus models
that just spit out tokens? KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: I

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko

With this new Flash, now we
are releasing the action API. So the models can do UI
actions on your computer by default. It's
on the API as well. We're bringing
everything together. So the models start looking
like an agent out of the box. But also at the same
time, what this enables is this enables building
really complicated systems that you want to
build a system that uses the model in different
way, maybe multiple models. That capability, I think,
is growing together. That's the frontier that I
think we will explore a lot. The direction that
the models will go is a lot more functionality
will be embedded in the model. So it's going to be
native for the model to be able to do a lot
of different things, to be able to use a lot of
different functionalities, your computer, your bash shell,
to be able to write code, build applications, and then
use those applications and all those kinds of things. There's a whole
exciting future there. But then with this
capability, there's the idea of what kind of systems
you can build that actually help people in particular domains. I think that is coming
because the models are becoming more and more capable. LOGAN KILPATRICK: I love that. Yeah, Josh? JOSH WOODWARD: I was going to
add from the Google Labs side on this, I think
it's exactly right. I think we were in
a phase where you're having to build a lot of
scaffolding around the model, and the model is,
over time, starting to do more and more
of that natively. And I think there's a huge
opportunity for the tooling and how you compose
some of these systems. So I'll just give

an 11-step process. That was kind of
an agentic system. We didn't call it that. We probably should have. But it was kind of
an under-the-hood way that you were able to
create that podcast dialogue between those two hosts. Now, a lot of that, we've been
able to compress and simplify.

experiences that can be built,

it's possible in a way. I think we're trying
to think about it less. There's been a
generation of products, I feel like, with AI where
AI was almost bolted on. And I think we're really
trying to think about workflows and the future of where
different, really industries are going. So what does the future of
software development look like? And that was yesterday. We had two products
start to go out. One was Jules and one
was called Stitch, if you came to the
developer keynote. And those are kind
of our hypotheses, really, of what it might
look like if you had a coding agent as a SWE, a
professional developer, and what it might look like if
you're starting from scratch, and you just want
to create a design and almost describe
how the design should work with no coding background. So usually, Logan, we think
about an area like that. We have a bunch of
very small teams, usually about 5 to 10 people. And we just go build. And we try to ship
as quick as we can. We put all these
experiments on labs.google. We see what works. Sometimes we have
something like a NotebookLM that just takes off. And we love those moments,
except our serving in capacity-- [LAUGHTER] --trying to find the TPUs. And sometimes we have
things that are duds and they don't work. And we put them to the
side and we move on. And I think that's
the ethos of trying to build a lot more
in public and iterate and really take user feedback. But I would say
the main takeaway on this question might
just be around thinking through the workflows of
your particular user type. And if it's a
developer or an artist or a filmmaker or
a musician, we try

But I would say
the main takeaway on this question might
just be around thinking through the workflows of
your particular user type. And if it's a

a musician, we try to co-create and get really

our product team, try to find the things
that make people grumpy. Because if you can
find that, there's usually a good creative
solution you can come up with. And if you do that really
well, they'll pay you for it. So that's the thought process we
go through when we're thinking of different new products. But it's sort of a numbers game. We've got a couple of dozen
things cooking right now, some are public and
some will be soon. LOGAN KILPATRICK:

organize the world's information besides Google, put

now, particularly, is it's not like, it didn't work

didn't work in 2.0, but maybe it now works in 2.5. Or maybe it doesn't
work in 2.5, but it's going to work in
the next version. I'm not going to guess what
the numbering system is. [LAUGHTER] I don't know what

going to work in

of jokes about the numbering internally, externally, anyway. But I think you have to
actually play with it. You have to play with
the tech and develop it. And then you have to say,
is my idea just not right or is it not right
for this time? And if it's not
right for this time, then you need to play
with the next version. And so I think the worst
cases are when you're like, we're going to pour two years
of brute force on the assumption that the frontier is here. That's actually going to be
less valuable than that ability to constantly adapt
and try new things. And I think if you take
Josh's audio podcast example, sometimes you're
adding more code, sometimes you're adding
more to the prompt, but actually sometimes
you're shrinking it down. You need it to do more. Don't just assume that the
next generation is more. It's like, can I
eliminate this step? Can I eliminate this extra data? Because now the models
are more powerful. And so the more agile
you are and the more I think you actually play with
both the tech and the product that you're building,
the more I think you'll be able to adapt at the
rate the world is adapting. LOGAN KILPATRICK:
That's wonderful advice. I think we're going to turn all

developers, Koray. There's lots of conversations
internally about the evals. And we're trying to
find the balance of what are the right evals for us,
as a company, building models and for DeepMind to hill climb
to make scientific progress on the models. And obviously
that's challenging. Developers have
the same challenge. It's really hard to know what

people who are building stuff

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
And obviously
that's challenging. Developers have
the same challenge. It's really hard to know what
success looks like if you're building an AI product. It's not easy to make evals. Do you have advice for
people who are building stuff from the lens of how you would
think about models if you were going and trying to build
a product, given what from how we do it with Gemini? KORAY KAVUKCUOGLU: That's
a challenging area, especially in AI nowadays. Because there are so
many different things that you can follow. I can imagine that
for developers, it's also hard to pick. Should I use this model? Should I use that model? How do I evaluate these models
under different kinds of things. I can, a little bit
explain what we try to do. And then hopefully it
translates to a lot of people. From the technology
development perspective, we look at three
main categories. One of them is academic evals. We look at these academic
evals because they give us a little bit
grounded, collaborated measure on the
capacity of the model. We know that those are not
exactly representative. There's a lot of actually
complicated science behind them in terms of understanding
how much of it is like potentially leaked in
different ways into the training data. So we try to be
really careful there, but we also try to
understand the actual signal we are getting from those. So we use those with a lot of
grain of salt, but we use those and we try to be careful there,
what we learn on that front. The second one is benchmarks,
leaderboards like LMSYS. And there are a
couple more as well, like WebDev Arena or
the Scale leaderboards and things like that. Those are important for
us because they are still calibrated, grounded,
all the models are there. And there are real users
trying real prompts. Of course, it's a
sort of a subset. Any product, your user base is
a subset of the real population. So the population that we
get from there, of course, has certain biases. They are very eager,
motivated about AI. They get to compare a
lot of different models.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
Any product, your user base is
a subset of the real population. So the population that we
get from there, of course, has certain biases. They are very eager,
motivated about AI. They get to compare a
lot of different models. The benefit that we get is
real users, real prompts, and that sort of
grounded comparison across different models. That gives us some signal. Because those are, as I
said, like real-world users, real-world prompts. And then the third
thing is the products. We work with Liz. We work with Josh and the team. And the signals that we get from
that are very important as well. Because there, we understand
a much broader community, especially in Search case,
a much broader user base. What are people doing? What are people really
paying attention to? What are the qualities of
the model that we really need to improve? Like factuality, grounding,
these kinds of things are really, really
important in both cases. But we had a lot of
constraints from there. How can you push
the frontier there? So balancing those
is really hard. I say that there's a little
bit of an alchemy in that. And that's your own
product motivation, what you're trying to build. I think at the end of the
day, that is important. Because it's really easy to
lock yourself into a situation that all the numbers
are coming at you and you don't
find a way to move. And what is important is,
what do you want to develop? And for Gemini, from
the model point of view, the important thing is we are
still trying to build AGI. We are doing that research. So amongst all these
signals we are getting, also it's really
important for us to always remember that we
are trying to build AGI. We are trying to push
a technology forward. And investing in those
areas that we believe in is quite important. So that's the balance. And I think one
of the main things that I would say
for developers is finding that mode that you have
that what is your product about. And always remember that
you have to try something.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – STMN5N-bDko
text:
So that's the balance. And I think one
of the main things that I would say
for developers is finding that mode that you have
that what is your product about. And always remember that
you have to try something. Because you are trying to get
the users you don't have right now, you don't know what
they are looking for. And they don't know
that you can provide it. ELIZABETH REID: Can
I add one statement? LOGAN KILPATRICK: Yeah, please. ELIZABETH REID: I think
it's really important to periodically step
back, relate to that, and ask how good your evals
are at giving you direction. I once was with an engineer
who gave this one, which is like, a lighthouse is
really good at getting you close to the island until
you get too close to the island. And then if you keep going
towards the lighthouse, you go away from it. And so you actually really have
to be thoughtful about this. Because the evals are
always proxies for what you're really trying to build. And if you get too focused
on just going after that eval and you forget that
it was a proxy, then you can be too focused on the
proxy and not the product. And so you regularly
should step back and say, is this pushing me forward? Is this eval as hard
as I need it to be? Is it reflecting how people
are changing in their usage? Is it overly focusing on
one thing and not another? And I think that's
essential because what people will expect of these
things is constantly changing. They are growing. They are asking more of it. And you want to make
sure you're always having evals that lead
you to the future, not that reinforce the past. LOGAN KILPATRICK: I love that. This has been an
awesome conversation. I feel like I love doing
this stuff because I get to sit here and learn
from people who have some of this stuff figured out. Let's do a couple
rapid fire ones. Liz, starting with
you, the future of Search in just a few words. We'll make it hard for you. ELIZABETH REID: OK,
just a few words. From information
to intelligence. LOGAN KILPATRICK: I like that. That's a good one.
